title: Psychology frameworks in practice
objective: Apply psychology's core method - operationalise a fuzzy idea, run a controlled comparison, separate correlation from causation, and test whether the result survives replication - to a real study.
beginnerExplanation: Psychology is the scientific study of mind and behaviour, and the thing that makes it a science is not the topics it studies but the method it uses. The central trick is operationalisation: turning a vague idea like 'obedience' or 'memory accuracy' into something you can actually count. Stanley Milgram could not measure 'obedience' directly, so in 1961 he operationalised it as the highest voltage a participant was willing to deliver to a stranger on the orders of an experimenter in a lab coat. The shocks were fake, the screaming 'learner' was an actor, but the dial readings were real data. About 65% of participants went all the way to the maximum 450-volt switch. That number only means something because Milgram defined, in advance, exactly what counted as the behaviour. When you read any psychology claim - 'gratitude makes you happier', 'violent games cause aggression' - the first question a practitioner asks is: how did they turn that abstract word into a number, and was the comparison fair?
deeperExplanation: The framework that runs underneath modern psychology is the controlled experiment plus the discipline of distinguishing correlation from causation. You take a measurable outcome, you change one thing (the independent variable) while holding everything else constant, and you randomly assign people to conditions so that pre-existing differences average out. Random assignment is the engine: it is the only thing that lets you say the manipulation caused the difference rather than merely travelled alongside it. A study showing that people who meditate are calmer proves nothing about meditation - calmer people may simply be more likely to take it up. Only randomly assigning some people to meditate and others not breaks that knot. Solomon Asch used this logic in the 1950s: he sat one real participant among actors who confidently gave an obviously wrong answer about line lengths, and measured how often the real person caved. Around a third of responses conformed to the wrong majority, and most participants conformed at least once - a clean demonstration of social pressure because the only thing that changed was the confederates' answers. The harder framework is honesty about what a result is worth. Effect sizes matter more than the famous 'p less than 0.05' threshold, because a real-but-tiny effect can be statistically significant in a big sample yet useless in life. And since roughly 2010, psychology has lived through a replication crisis: when large teams re-ran celebrated findings, many failed to reproduce. 'Ego depletion' - the idea that willpower is a limited fuel that runs down - and several social-priming results are leading examples that wobbled or collapsed under direct replication. This is not psychology failing; it is psychology applying its own framework to itself. The leading view now is that small samples, flexible analysis, and publication bias inflated the original effects. Applying frameworks in practice means treating a single striking study as a hypothesis, not a fact, until it replicates.
keyTakeaways: Operationalisation is step one: a usable psychology study defines in advance exactly what behaviour counts (Milgram counted maximum voltage delivered, not the feeling of 'obedience').
Random assignment is what licenses a causal claim - it is the only thing that turns 'these two things go together' into 'this one caused that one'.
Correlation is not causation: meditators being calmer could mean meditation works, or that calm people meditate; only a randomised comparison can tell them apart.
Effect size beats statistical significance - a real effect can be both 'significant' and too small to matter in real life.
Treat a single famous study as a hypothesis, not a fact: the post-2010 replication crisis showed celebrated findings like ego depletion can fail to reproduce.
Strong claims need converging evidence - one lab, one sample, one analysis is the start of an argument, not the end of it.
example: Take Elizabeth Loftus's misinformation work as a worked case of the framework in action. The fuzzy question is 'how reliable is memory?'. Loftus operationalised it: show people a video of a car crash, then split them randomly into groups that get differently worded questions. One group is asked how fast the cars were going when they 'hit'; another when they 'smashed into' each other. The single word is the independent variable; everything else is held constant. The 'smashed' group reported higher speeds, and a week later were more likely to falsely 'remember' broken glass that was never in the film. Because assignment was random, the wording - not some difference between the people - caused the false memory. The practical payload is enormous: it is part of why eyewitness identification, once treated as gold-standard evidence, is now known to have helped convict innocent people. Memory is reconstructive, not a recording, and the framework is what proved it.
commonMistakes: Reading a correlation as a cause - 'students who sleep more get better grades' does not establish that sleep raises grades; conscientious students may both sleep well and study well.

Trusting one dramatic study because it is famous - the Stanford Prison Experiment (Zimbardo, 1971) is endlessly cited yet is now heavily criticised on methodological grounds, including coached guards and a tiny non-random sample.
Confusing statistical significance with importance - a result can clear 'p less than 0.05' in a large sample while the actual effect is too small to change anything.

Treating psychoanalytic-style claims as tested science - Freud was historically enormous but most of his specific theories are not framed so they can be tested or falsified, which is a different thing from being proven.
exercise: Find a psychology claim in the news this week (a headline like 'standing desks make teams more creative'). Reverse-engineer the framework: (1) How would you operationalise the outcome - what exactly would you count? (2) What is the independent variable, and could people be randomly assigned to it? (3) Is the reported relationship correlational or causal, and which words in the article hide that distinction? (4) Has it been replicated, or is it a single study? Write one sentence for each. If you cannot answer (2) and (4), treat the claim as an interesting hypothesis, not a finding.

mentalModel.title: Turn the fuzzy word into a fair comparison
mentalModel.summary: Every defensible psychology finding starts by converting an abstract idea (obedience, memory, willpower) into something countable, then changes one variable at a time with people randomly assigned, so that any difference can be pinned on the manipulation rather than on who the participants already were.
anchors: operationalisation - define exactly what behaviour counts before you collect data
random assignment - the engine that converts correlation into a causal claim
replication - a result you trust is one that survives being re-run by other people

howItWorks.title: From hunch to defensible claim
steps: Name the fuzzy concept you care about (for example, 'social pressure changes what people say').
Operationalise it into a measurable outcome (Asch counted how often a person agreed with confederates giving an obviously wrong answer).
Isolate one independent variable and randomly assign people to its levels, holding everything else constant.
Report the effect size, not just whether it cleared significance - ask 'how big, in real terms?'
Withhold belief until it replicates: a finding that only one lab can produce is a lead, not a law.

upsides: The method catches self-deception: random assignment exposes 'effects' that were really just pre-existing differences between groups.
It produced findings that genuinely changed institutions - Loftus's memory work reshaped how courts treat eyewitness testimony.
downsides: Lab operationalisations can drift from real life - the maximum-voltage switch is a clean number but a strange stand-in for everyday moral choices.
The same machinery, applied loosely with small samples and flexible analysis, manufactured the very findings the replication crisis later overturned.
teaching.workedExample: A celebrated 1990s result claimed that reading words about old age (grey, wrinkle, Florida) made people walk more slowly out of the room - 'social priming'. Apply the framework. The operationalisation is fine: walking speed down a corridor, timed. But when independent teams re-ran it with larger samples and blind timing (so the experimenter could not nudge the result), the slow-walking effect largely vanished. The framework's verdict: the original effect size was probably inflated by a small sample and an experimenter who knew which condition each person was in. The lesson is not 'psychology is fake' - it is that the same method that found the effect is the method that correctly retired it. A claim is only as strong as its most careful replication.
realWorldApplications: Eyewitness testimony reform in courts, driven by Loftus's false-memory experiments
Behavioural economics and 'nudge' policy, built on Kahneman and Tversky's System 1 / System 2 model of fast versus slow thinking
Randomised controlled trials for therapies such as CBT, which use the same control-group logic to test whether a treatment actually works
A/B testing in product and marketing teams - the same random-assignment experiment, applied to button colours and emails
whoWorksWithThis: Research psychologist / academic - designs the experiments and fights over operationalisation and replication
Clinical psychologist - reads the trial evidence to choose treatments that actually outperform a control condition
Organisational psychologist - runs controlled studies on hiring, motivation, and team performance inside companies
Neuropsychologist - links measurable behaviour to brain function, often after injury, using the same controlled-comparison discipline

quiz.question: A study finds that people who drink more coffee report more anxiety. What can you conclude?
options: Nothing causal - this is a correlation, and only random assignment to coffee-or-not could test causation
quiz.explanation: This is a correlation. Coffee might raise anxiety, anxious people might self-medicate or stay up drinking coffee, or a third factor (long work hours) could drive both. Only randomly assigning people to drink coffee or not, holding everything else constant, can isolate cause from coincidence.

quiz.question: Why did findings like 'ego depletion' become central to the replication crisis?
options: When large independent teams re-ran the studies, the celebrated effects often shrank or disappeared, suggesting the originals were inflated by small samples and flexible analysis
quiz.explanation: Most replication failures are not fraud. They reflect small original samples, publication bias toward exciting results, and flexible analysis. When other labs ran tighter, larger versions, many effects faded. This is psychology correctly applying its own method to itself.

perspectives.researchPsychologist: My whole job lives or dies on operationalisation. I will spend a week arguing about how to measure 'trust' before I run a single participant, because if the measure is sloppy the prettiest result means nothing. And since the replication reckoning, I pre-register my hypotheses and analysis plan - I commit in writing to what counts as success before I see the data, so I can't fool myself after the fact.
perspectives.clinicalPsychologist: I don't pick therapies because they sound profound; I pick the ones that beat a control group in randomised trials. That is why I lean on CBT for many anxiety presentations - it has been tested against waitlists and placebos repeatedly. But I also hold it loosely: I watch effect sizes, not headlines, because a treatment that 'works' on average can still be wrong for the person in front of me.
perspectives.organisationalPsychologist: Inside a company everyone has a confident theory about what motivates people, and most of them are correlations dressed up as causes. My value is running the actual controlled comparison - randomly assign two versions of an onboarding flow, measure retention, and let the data settle the argument. It is the Asch and Loftus logic, just pointed at hiring and team performance instead of line lengths and car crashes.
